---
title: "Bayesian Spatial Synthetic Control"
subtitle: "California's Proposition 99, three estimators, and a Nevada-sized spillover"
deck-author: "Carlos Mendez"
deck-author-url: "https://carlos-mendez.org"
institute: "Nagoya University (GSID)"
date: today
date-format: long
key-results:
  - num: "−18.46"
    cap: "ATT · packs per capita per year"
  - num: "−3.75"
    cap: "Nevada spillover · SUTVA fails"
  - num: "0.223"
    cap: "spatial autocorrelation rho"
format:
  revealjs:
    output-file: index.html
    # NOT embed-resources: RevealChalkboard (a core feature) is incompatible with
    # self-contained output, so the deck ships index.html + slides_files/ (Hugo serves
    # this fine — the tutorial.html precedent). Figures are referenced ../r_sc_bayes_spatial_*.png.
    theme: [default, site-brand.scss]
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    center: true
    slide-number: c/t
    menu: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
    overview: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    fig-align: center
    auto-stretch: true
    highlight-style: github
    # Math: MathJax (Quarto revealjs default) — renders $…$ / $$…$$ across the whole slide
    # DOM (body, captions, headings, table cells). Loads from a CDN; reveal.js is local.
    # Do NOT set `html-math-method: katex` — it is broken in revealjs (no auto-render call,
    # so math shows as raw LaTeX). See references/render-and-fix.md.
    link-external-icon: false
---

# The Tension {.divider background-color="#d97757"}

[Act I]{.act}

## Two assumptions decide whether you trust the most-studied policy in econometrics

Prop 99 raised California's cigarette tax 25 cents in 1988. The classic answer — a 25–30 pack drop — has been quoted for 20 years.

. . .

But it rests on two quiet assumptions: donor weights live on a *sparse simplex*, and donor states are *unaffected* by California's policy. *What if both are wrong?*

::: {.notes}
This is the central tension. Classical SCM gives a clean headline, but the simplex constraint may manufacture its four-donor sparsity, and SUTVA may be violated by Californians driving to Nevada for cheaper cigarettes. We will test both — and reject one.
:::

## Same data, three estimators, one ATT — but the donor pool quadruples

![ATT stays −18 to −16 packs/capita across all three estimators; active donors climb 4 → 23 → 27.](../r_sc_bayes_spatial_01_classical_paths.png)

::: {.notes}
Spoiler figure. Don't explain the gap yet — just plant that the headline ATT survives all three specifications, while the synthetic California's *composition* does not. We earn each estimator in Act II and return to this picture.
:::

## Where we're going

::: {.incremental}
- The case: California, Prop 99, and the SUTVA problem
- Three nested estimators — classical → horseshoe → spatial
- The ATT (the estimand) and how its credible interval moves
- The spillover that proves SUTVA is empirically false
:::

# The Investigation {.divider background-color="#6a9bcc"}

[Act II]{.act}

## The estimand is the ATT for one treated unit: California

$$\mathrm{ATT} = E\big[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) \mid D_i = 1\big]$$

With a single treated unit, the ATT is the gap between *observed* California and a *counterfactual* California, averaged over 1988–2000.

[Every stage targets the **same** ATT — only the way it builds the counterfactual California changes.]{.takeaway .fragment}

::: {.notes}
Be explicit about the estimand: this is the average treatment effect on the treated, not on the population. We never observe untreated California after 1988, so we construct a synthetic twin from donor states. The whole deck is about how that twin is built.
:::

## The lab: a balanced 39-state panel, 1970–2000, 1,209 rows

::: {.incremental}
- **Outcome** — per-capita cigarette sales (`cigsale`)
- **Treated** — California; treatment switches on in 1988
- **Donors** — the other 38 states, untreated throughout
- **Pre / post** — 18 pre-treatment years, 13 post-treatment years
:::

[Spatial structure ships with the data: a 38×38 binary contiguity matrix $W$ among donors, row-normalized before it enters the SAR likelihood.]{.takeaway .fragment}

## Three estimators relax one assumption at a time

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Stage 1 — Classical

- simplex weights (Abadie 2010)
- SUTVA imposed
- `tidysynth`
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Stages 2–3 — Bayesian

- horseshoe prior on weights
- SAR layer drops SUTVA
- C++ Gibbs samplers
:::
::::

[Each arrow weakens an assumption: Stage 1 imposes simplex + SUTVA; Stage 2 relaxes the simplex; Stage 3 also relaxes SUTVA.]{.takeaway .fragment}

::: {.notes}
The pipeline is the post's Mermaid diagram, re-expressed as columns: classical → Bayesian horseshoe → Bayesian spatial SAR, then diagnostics and cross-stage comparison. The pedagogical point is what *moves* between stages.
:::

## With zero substantive controls, the simplex picks just four donors

$$\widehat\alpha = \arg\min_\alpha \big\| Y_{1,\text{pre}} - Y_{c,\text{pre}}\,\alpha \big\|^2 \quad \text{s.t.}\quad \alpha_j \ge 0,\ \sum_j \alpha_j = 1$$

[The non-negativity-plus-sum-to-one constraint is an implicit regularizer — it pushes most weights to exactly zero.]{.takeaway .fragment}

::: {.notes}
Read the equation aloud: match California's pre-treatment sales with a convex combination of donors. The simplex serves interpretability, but it also forces sparsity. Is the four-donor synthetic the data talking, or the constraint?
:::

## Classical SCM concentrates 97.5% of the weight on four states

| Donor | Weight |
|---|---:|
| Utah | [0.327]{.key} |
| Nevada | 0.255 |
| Montana | 0.245 |
| Connecticut | 0.148 |

[The other 34 donors are essentially zero — a near-pure four-state synthetic California.]{.takeaway .fragment}

::: {.notes}
Utah, Nevada, Montana, Connecticut carry 97.5% of the weight. Two questions hang over this: is the sparsity real, and is Nevada — California's neighbour — contaminated by spillovers? Stages 2 and 3 attack each.
:::

## Classical SCM lands the ATT at −18.46 packs per capita {background-color="#141413"}

[−18.46]{.bignum}

[ATT, classical SCM (95% bootstrap CI [−22.21, −14.45]) · smaller than Abadie's −27 because the shipped predictors are leaner]{.bignum-label}

::: {.notes}
The interval never crosses zero. Our −18.46 is below Abadie's ≈ −27 because the shipped data only carries cigsale and retprice — no log income, youth share, or beer sales. The pipeline is identical; the inputs are leaner. The qualitative finding survives.
:::

## The horseshoe prior keeps zero overwhelmingly likely — yet lets a few donors escape

$$\alpha_j \mid \tau, \lambda_j \sim \mathcal{N}\big(0,\ \tau^2 \lambda_j^2\big), \quad \lambda_j \sim \mathcal{C}^+(0,1), \quad \tau \sim \mathcal{C}^+(0,1)$$

A global scale $\tau$ pulls everything toward zero; a *local* scale $\lambda_j$ lets individual donors break free. The half-Cauchy tails do the rest.

[Now the **data**, not the constraint, decide which donors get non-zero mass.]{.takeaway .fragment}

::: {.notes}
The horseshoe replaces the simplex with a soft, heavy-tailed regularizer. Most weights hug zero; a handful escape. This is the test of whether the four-donor sparsity was real.
:::

## Relax the simplex and the donor pool jumps from 4 to 23 active states

![Posterior mean donor weights under the horseshoe, with 95% credible intervals — most hug zero, but only Nevada's interval clears zero.](../r_sc_bayes_spatial_02_horseshoe_weights.png)

::: {.notes}
23 of 38 donors now carry mean weight above 0.01. Connecticut leads (0.218), but only Nevada's 95% credible interval [0.081, 0.266] excludes zero. The lesson: classical "sparsity" is partly a constraint artifact — the data do not insist on four donors.
:::

## Propagating weight uncertainty widens the band but never reaches zero

![California vs horseshoe-posterior-mean synthetic (top) and the gap with a 95% credible band (bottom) — the band widens post-1988 but stays below zero.](../r_sc_bayes_spatial_05_stage2_paths.png)

::: {.notes}
ATT moves to −15.84, CrI [−21.76, −9.48] — wider than Stage 1 by design, because the horseshoe carries weight uncertainty into the gap rather than fixing weights at the optimizer's best guess. Pre-1988 fit is excellent; the post-band reaches ±10 by 1995. Still no zero crossing.
:::

## Drop SUTVA: each donor's sales depend on its neighbours' sales

$$Y_{c,t} = \rho\, W\, Y_{c,t} + X_{c,t}\beta + Y_c^{\text{lag}}\alpha + \varepsilon_t$$

The spatial lag $W Y_{c,t}$ is the row-normalized neighbour average; $\rho$ measures how strongly a state co-moves with its neighbours. When $\rho = 0$ we recover Stage 2.

[If $\rho > 0$, a donor's sales are partly its neighbours' — so Nevada's post-1988 sales are part of the **treatment effect**, not the counterfactual.]{.takeaway .fragment}

::: {.notes}
This is where SUTVA is dropped. The SAR layer lets California's policy diffuse into adjacent donors through W. The cross-border-shopping literature predicts exactly this for the California–Nevada line.
:::

## The Gibbs pipeline runs both MCMCs and the spillovers in one call

``` {.r code-line-numbers="1-3|5-8|10-13"}
w <- as.matrix(california_smoking$w[, 2])    # CA's contiguity row
W <- as.matrix(california_smoking$W[, -1])   # 38x38 donor contiguity
rownames(W) <- colnames(W) <- california_smoking$W$state

fit_sar <- sc_spillover(
  data = panel_df, treated_unit = "California",
  w = w, W = W, y = "cigsale", X = c("retprice"),
  M = MCMC_ITER, burn = MCMC_BURN, seed = SEED)   # horseshoe + SAR

rho_hat    <- fit_sar$rho_hat                  # spatial autocorrelation
att_sar    <- fit_sar$effects$ate_point        # the ATT (estimand)
att_sar_ci <- fit_sar$effects$ate_ci95         # posterior credible interval
```

::: {.notes}
sc_spillover runs the horseshoe-alpha and SAR-rho samplers and post-processes the per-state spillovers in one call. The full C++ kernels live in the post's helpers; here we show the load-bearing lines. The ATT comes from the posterior, with a credible interval — the Bayesian posterior over the effect path is the deliverable.
:::

## The posterior puts spatial autocorrelation at rho = 0.223, clearly above zero {background-color="#141413"}

[0.223]{.bignum}

[posterior mean $\hat\rho$ (95% CrI [0.168, 0.272]) · moderate, stable, and bounded away from zero]{.bignum-label}

::: {.notes}
A 1-unit change in the neighbour-averaged sales moves own sales 0.223 units, controlling for the horseshoe role and retprice. Modest but unambiguously non-zero. The Stage 3 ATT lands at −16.59, between classical and horseshoe — the SAR layer reattributes a sliver of the gap to neighbour diffusion.
:::

## The posterior effect path widens linearly from −5 in 1988 to −27 by 2000

![California observed vs SAR-corrected synthetic (top) and the treatment-effect path over time (bottom) — the effect on California deepens roughly linearly after 1988.](../r_sc_bayes_spatial_06_stage3_paths.png)

::: {.notes}
The Bayesian posterior gives an effect *path*, not just a single ATT. It widens from about −5 packs/capita in 1988 to about −27 by 2000 — steeper than the cumulative mean implies, because the SAR layer attributes part of the early gap to spillover diffusion rather than California's own response. By the late 1990s the per-year effect on California alone exceeds the classical headline.
:::

## Almost the entire spillover lands on one state: Nevada

![Top-8 donor states by absolute post-1988 spillover — Nevada's −3.75 dwarfs every other state.](../r_sc_bayes_spatial_03_spillover_effects.png)

::: {.notes}
Nevada absorbs −3.75 packs/capita — 16× the next state (Idaho, −0.228) and more than 2,900× the smallest non-zero spillover (North Dakota). Nevada is California's eastern neighbour, the only donor with a substantial contiguity link, so the row-normalized W concentrates the spillover mass there. This is the empirical signature of SUTVA failure.
:::

## Before trusting the posterior, the prior must be compatible with the data

![Prior predictive check on four summary statistics — all four observed orange lines land inside the simulated prior cloud, not in its tails.](../r_sc_bayes_spatial_04_prior_predictive.png)

::: {.notes}
1,000 joint prior draws are forward-simulated and compared to the observed donor panel on the mean, the spatial quadratic form y'Wy, lag-1 autocorrelation, and PC1 variance share. All four observed values land inside the cloud — the prior informs, it does not overwhelm. Had they landed in the tails, the posterior would be prior-driven.
:::

# The Resolution {.divider background-color="#00d4c8"}

[Act III]{.act}

## All three estimators agree on sign and scale: Prop 99 worked

| Stage | ATT | 95% Interval | Active donors |
|---|---:|---|---:|
| Classical SCM | [−18.46]{.key} | [−22.21, −14.45] | 4 |
| Bayesian HS | −15.84 | [−21.76, −9.48] | 23 |
| Bayesian Spatial SAR | −16.59 | [−16.78, −16.39] | 27 |

[The headline ATT is robust; the donor pool's *shape* is not — and the Stage-3 interval is artificially narrow (ESS(ρ) = 3 at tutorial scale).]{.takeaway .fragment}

::: {.notes}
Three reads. The sign and order of magnitude agree across all three — none of the intervals reaches zero. The active-donor count rises 4 → 23 → 27 as priors relax, so the sparse four-donor synthetic is one of many plausible counterfactuals. The Stage-3 CrI is the narrowest but least trustworthy because rho has not mixed at 5,000 iterations.
:::

## Does the SAR layer make this causal? No — two assumptions still carry the weight

[Objection.]{.objection} A spatial model and machine-selected controls can't manufacture identification.

. . .

[Response.]{.rebuttal} Correct. The ATT is identified only under conditional independence given $X$ and parallel trends. The horseshoe just selects controls flexibly; the SAR layer just *tests* SUTVA — and rejects it. We evaluate a *method*, not a naive causal claim.

::: {.notes}
Steelman, don't strawman. Bayesian machinery disciplines selection and surfaces spillovers; it does not relax the core identifying assumptions. The contribution is showing that SUTVA is testable and, here, false — which widens the policy's measured reach.
:::

## SUTVA is empirically false here — and that widens Prop 99's true reach {background-color="#141413"}

[−3.75]{.bignum}

[Nevada's post-treatment spillover (packs/capita) · 16× the next donor · direct evidence SUTVA is violated]{.bignum-label}

::: {.notes}
The policy takeaway: reporting only California's −18 understates Prop 99. The tax reshaped consumption on both sides of the California–Nevada border. When a treated unit has one or two highly-exposed neighbours, the classical "donors are unaffected" assumption can be tested — and here it fails decisively.
:::

# Let the data, not the simplex, choose your donors — and let the map tell you who else was treated. {.divider background-color="#141413"}

::: {.notes}
The single takeaway. The headline effect is robust to every prior structure, but the synthetic's composition is not, and SUTVA is false: Prop 99's reach crosses the Nevada border. Relax the simplex to see the uncertainty; add the spatial layer to see the spillover.
:::
